deconstruction_targets: The idea of equal/differential practice among devils
constructive_proposals: Establishing a science of reality based on principles opposed to Arabian Aristotelianism
Asserting hierarchical order among devils (interior and exterior actions)
Defining divine wisdom as permitting evil for greater good
Proposing that higher angels have authority over lower devils due to proximity to God

scapegoat.name: Devils
attributes: without distinction in sin and punishment
scapegoat.justification: Devils are portrayed as inherently disordered, morally corrupt entities whose actions stem from sin and punishment rather than nature. They are collectively condemned for practicing 'filthy' acts (incubus and succubus) without distinction, framed as disorderly and opposed to divine order.

moral_justification: The text justifies moral hierarchy by asserting that devils lack the nobility of higher angels and thus are inherently prone to evil. Evil is permitted by Divine wisdom for a greater good, but devils are held responsible through their nature as impure and prideful spirits who freely choose disorder over obedience.
summary: This passage deconstructs the idea of uniformity among devils by asserting hierarchical order based on divine hierarchy and moral worth. It condemns devils as inherently sinful and disordered, using theological authority (Augustine, Thomas, Dionysus) to justify differential punishment and subordination. While it offers constructive elements like a structured cosmology and role for good angels in permitting evil, its primary mode is deconstructive — dismantling egalitarian interpretations of demonic behavior through moral condemnation and hierarchical theology.
